content.problemStatement:
A binary string is a string consisting of 000 and 111. We define an island as a substring consisting of all 000s bounded by 111s (or the ends of the string).

For example, in the string 100010000100010000100010000, there are 222 islands; 000000000 (bounded by 111s) and 000000000000 (bounded by 111 and end of string). Note that the substring 000000 in the above string is not an island.

You are given positive integers NNN and KKK. Find whether there exists a binary string of length NNN such that:


There are **exactly** KKK islands in the string;
Each island has a **distinct** length.

Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two space-separated integers NNN and KKK — the length of binary string and the required number of islands.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line, `YES` if there exist valid binary string, otherwise print `NO`.


You may print each character of the string in uppercase or lowercase (for example, the strings `YES`, `yEs`, `yes`, and `yeS` will all be treated as identical).
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 2⋅1062\cdot 10^62⋅106.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: The only binary string satisfying the conditions is 000 as it has length 111 and contains one island, i.e. 000 itself.
Test case 222: Consider the binary string 001001001 of length 333. The only island in this string is 000000.
Some other valid binary strings under conditions are 000,101,000, 101,000,101, and 110110110. The strings 111,010111, 010111,010 are not valid.
Test case 333: It is not possible to generate a binary string under given conditions.
Test case 444: Consider the binary string 101100010110001011000 of length 777. There are exactly two islands with lengths 111 and 333.
Note that strings like 0001000,10100100001000, 10100100001000,1010010 are not valid.
